stories.headline: Update: Iran Offers U.S. Deal to Reopen Strait of Hormuz Without Nuclear Terms; Israel Deploys Iron Dome to UAE
stories.summary:
Iran has passed a new proposal to the United States through Pakistan that would reopen the Strait of Hormuz and end the two-month-old war without addressing Tehran's nuclear program, according to a U.S. official and two sources with knowledge of the offer. The proposal would bypass the deeply divisive question of nuclear concessions, which has been the central sticking point in diplomacy, in exchange for the U.S. lifting its own blockade and halting military operations against Iran.

President Trump appears unlikely to accept the offer, as doing so would remove his primary leverage over Iran's nuclear ambitions — the very issue that prompted the U.S. and Israel to launch military operations on February 28. The Strait of Hormuz blockade has driven oil prices sharply higher and rattled global markets, with the Chevron CEO warning of continued "upward pressure" on energy prices. German Chancellor Friedrich Merz warned publicly that the U.S. risks becoming bogged down in a quagmire similar to Iraq and Afghanistan.

In a significant new military development first reported Monday, Israel has deployed an Iron Dome air defense system, along with troops to operate it, to the United Arab Emirates early in the war — an unprecedented step reflecting deepening Israel-UAE security cooperation as both countries face Iranian threats. The deployment had not been previously made public.

Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi traveled to Moscow to meet with Russian President Vladimir Putin, seeking political backing as peace talks with Washington remain on hold. Pakistan and Oman are continuing backchannel efforts to prevent a return to open hostilities, while analysts say both sides remain unlikely to resume large-scale fighting in the near term despite the diplomatic deadlock.

stories.headline: United Airlines CEO Formally Withdraws Proposed Merger With American Airlines
stories.summary:
United Airlines CEO Scott Kirby officially took a proposed merger with American Airlines off the table Monday, after the deal was rejected by American and met with broad opposition from antitrust experts, lawmakers, and President Trump. Kirby had floated the combination during a private meeting with Trump in late February, but the proposal quickly generated criticism that it would further consolidate an already highly concentrated industry, reduce flight routes, and raise ticket prices for consumers.

American Airlines CEO Robert Isom had called the merger "a nonstarter from the get-go" on antitrust grounds, and the airline said it was "not engaged with or interested in any discussions" about the deal. Trump, despite his administration's generally business-friendly posture, expressed concern that a combined United-American would become "lazy" and said he would be open to other configurations.

Kirby defended the concept in his statement, arguing the combination would have expanded the global footprint of U.S. carriers and helped close a competitive gap with foreign airlines, which he said fly roughly 65 percent of long-haul seats into the U.S. even though only 40 percent of passengers are foreign citizens. He maintained that federal regulators would have approved the deal had American been a willing partner.

The collapse of the proposed merger leaves open the question of whether the current tariff and trade environment — which has disrupted international aviation demand — will prompt further consolidation talks elsewhere in the airline industry.

stories.headline: China Blocks Meta From Acquiring Chinese AI Startup Manus
stories.summary:
Chinese regulators have blocked Meta from acquiring Manus, a Chinese artificial intelligence startup, in a decision that could send a significant warning signal to Chinese tech founders considering partnerships or sales to foreign companies. Meta said the transaction "complied fully with applicable law" and expressed confidence it would reach "an appropriate resolution to the inquiry."

The ruling comes amid heightened geopolitical tensions between the U.S. and China, and as both governments have increasingly scrutinized cross-border technology deals involving artificial intelligence. The full impact of the decision was not immediately clear, but analysts say it could chill future deals between Chinese AI firms and Western acquirers at a moment when AI competition between the two countries is intensifying.

Manus had attracted attention as a capable AI agent system developed in China, and its potential acquisition by Meta would have represented a significant cross-border AI technology transaction. The blocking of the deal reflects Beijing's growing assertiveness in controlling the flow of advanced technology assets to foreign entities.

stories.headline: Update: UK's Starmer to Face Parliamentary Vote on Investigation Into Mandelson Misleading-Parliament Claims
stories.summary:
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer is facing a parliamentary vote on whether to launch a formal investigation into allegations that he misled Parliament, setting up a high-stakes loyalty test for his Labour Party just days before crucial local elections across England. The controversy centers on claims related to Peter Mandelson, the veteran Labour figure appointed as UK ambassador to Washington, whose conduct has drawn sustained scrutiny.

The vote represents a significant political test for Starmer, who has already faced questions about his handling of the Mandelson appointment and is navigating a difficult period for Labour ahead of May local elections where the party is expected to suffer historic losses in Wales, according to earlier coverage. A formal investigation finding that a prime minister misled Parliament would be a serious constitutional and political matter under Westminster conventions.

The development adds pressure to a Labour government already managing tensions over its relationship with the Trump administration in Washington and domestic dissatisfaction with its economic record. Opposition parties are expected to unite behind the motion calling for an inquiry, making the outcome dependent on Labour backbench discipline at a vulnerable moment for the party.

stories.headline: Update: Iran Nuclear Talks at Impasse as Araghchi Flies to Moscow; Pakistan Lifts Security Lockdown
stories.summary:
Iran-U.S. peace talks collapsed over the weekend after a planned second round of direct negotiations in Islamabad failed to materialize. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi made a brief return visit to Pakistan before departing for Moscow to meet with President Vladimir Putin, seeking to build diplomatic leverage as the conflict stretches into its third month. Pakistan's foreign minister lifted the security lockdown around the Islamabad high-security zone, signaling no imminent resumption of direct talks.

President Trump, speaking on Fox News, dismissed the need to send envoys on a 17-hour flight, saying: 'We have all the cards. If they want to talk, they can come to us, or they can call us.' Iran's foreign ministry blamed Washington for the breakdown. Indirect back-channel efforts continue, with Pakistani and Omani officials conveying messages between the two sides.

Araghchi's meeting with Putin is aimed at coordinating strategy and securing Russian backing at a moment when U.S.-Iran negotiations are effectively frozen. Iran has separately proposed a deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz without addressing its nuclear program — a framework the U.S. has not publicly accepted. Oil prices have risen sharply in response to the diplomatic impasse.

Trump also expressed sharp disappointment with NATO, saying member nations failed to assist when Iran restricted the Strait of Hormuz. Energy analysts warn that jet fuel inventories in several regions remain at critically low levels, with Chevron's CEO publicly warning of continued upward pressure on oil prices.

stories.headline: Kim Jong Un Unveils Memorial for North Korean Soldiers Killed Fighting in Ukraine
stories.summary:
North Korean leader Kim Jong Un and Russian Defense Minister Andrey Belousov jointly unveiled a memorial museum in Pyongyang on Sunday honoring North Korean soldiers who died fighting alongside Russian forces in Ukraine. The ceremony included military jet flyovers and the release of white balloons. The memorial, called the Museum of Combat Feats at the Overseas Military Operations, coincided with the first anniversary of Russia's declared recapture of parts of the Kursk region.

South Korean intelligence estimates that approximately 15,000 North Korean troops have been deployed to help Russia and that roughly 2,000 have been killed — figures neither Pyongyang nor Moscow has confirmed. Kim pledged continued full support for Russia's military campaign, declaring that Russia 'will surely win a victory in the just sacred war.' Belousov said he discussed long-term military cooperation with North Korean officials during the visit.

The public ceremony represents a significant escalation in the open acknowledgment of the North Korea-Russia military partnership, which had previously been conducted with considerable opacity. In exchange for troops, North Korea is believed to have received food, financial transfers, and technical military assistance from Moscow.

The event deepens concerns among Western governments and South Korea about the institutionalization of a Russia-North Korea defense axis. The two countries signed a mutual defense treaty in June 2024, and the memorial's opening signals both nations' intent to formally commemorate and legitimize the deployment of North Korean forces abroad.

stories.headline: Colombia Offers Record $1.4 Million Reward for Rebel Leader Behind Deadly Bus Bombing That Killed 20
stories.summary:
Colombia's Defense Minister Pedro Sánchez announced a record reward of 5 billion pesos — approximately $1.4 million — for information leading to the capture of rebel leader 'Marlon,' whose real name is Iván Jacob Idrobo Arredondo, following Saturday's bomb attack on a bus traveling along the Pan-American Highway that killed 20 civilians and injured 36 others. The blast opened a massive crater in the highway connecting Cali and Popayán in the southwestern Cauca region, destroying several buses and vehicles.

'Marlon' is a senior figure in an armed dissident group led by Iván Mordisco, Colombia's most wanted man and a former FARC commander who broke from the peace process. President Gustavo Petro described the perpetrators as 'terrorists, fascists and drug traffickers' and ordered additional troops to the region. Cauca Governor Octavio Guzmán called it 'the most brutal and ruthless attack against the civilian population in decades.'

The attack comes just over a month before Colombia's presidential election on May 31, injecting the security situation into an already polarized campaign. Petro's preferred successor, left-wing candidate Iván Cepeda, has advocated for renewed negotiations with rebel groups, while right-wing opposition candidates are calling for a tougher military approach. Opinion polls currently show Cepeda leading the field.

The bombing underscores the fragility of Colombia's post-2016 security environment, where multiple dissident factions that rejected the FARC peace accord continue to operate and have intensified attacks in southwestern provinces. The region has seen a series of violent incidents over the same weekend.

stories.headline: Supreme Court Hears Bayer-Roundup Cancer Liability Case That Could Shield Companies From State Lawsuits
stories.summary:
The U.S. Supreme Court heard oral arguments Monday in a closely watched case involving Bayer, the German pharmaceutical and agricultural giant and parent company of Monsanto, over whether thousands of state-court lawsuits alleging that its Roundup weedkiller causes cancer are preempted by federal pesticide law. A ruling in Bayer's favor could effectively end mass litigation that has already cost the company billions of dollars in settlements and verdicts.

The case centers on glyphosate, the active ingredient in Roundup, which has been linked to cancer — particularly non-Hodgkin's lymphoma — in some studies, though the Environmental Protection Agency has repeatedly concluded it is unlikely to be carcinogenic when used as directed. Plaintiffs argue that Bayer failed to adequately warn consumers of potential cancer risks; Bayer contends that EPA-approved labeling preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims.

The case has drawn attention from the Make America Healthy Again movement aligned with the Trump administration, which has focused on chemical exposures in consumer products. A ruling broadly preempting state tort claims could have sweeping implications not only for Roundup litigation but for consumer product liability cases across industries, limiting the ability of injured parties to seek damages in state courts.

Bayer faces tens of thousands of pending lawsuits in the United States related to Roundup. The company has already paid approximately $10 billion in settlements but has been unable to resolve the full litigation. A decision favorable to the company could halt the remaining cases, while a ruling against it would expose Bayer to continued and potentially growing legal liability. A ruling is expected by the end of June.

stories.headline: Update: WHCD Shooter Cole Tomas Allen to Appear in Federal Court; Motive Probed as Administration Officials Targeted
stories.summary:
Cole Tomas Allen, 31, of Torrance, California, is expected to appear in federal court Monday on charges stemming from Saturday's shooting at the White House Correspondents' Association Dinner at the Washington Hilton. Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said Allen is believed to have been targeting President Trump and senior administration officials, and that Allen told law enforcement as much. Allen sent a lengthy manifesto to family members approximately ten minutes before the attack, in which he referred to himself as a 'Friendly Federal Assassin' and described Trump as a 'traitor.'

Blanche said Allen 'barely breached the perimeter' before being subdued by Secret Service, calling the response 'a massive security success story.' One Secret Service agent was struck but protected by body armor. Trump, who was inside the ballroom performing a mentalist trick with a guest when shots rang out, was evacuated along with First Lady Melania Trump, Cabinet officials, and Vice President Vance. Trump later told CBS News's Norah O'Donnell in a '60 Minutes' interview that he 'wasn't worried,' while sharply criticizing O'Donnell for reading portions of the alleged manifesto on air.

The incident has triggered debates on multiple fronts. Republican officials and Trump himself have used the shooting to press the case for a proposed $400 million White House ballroom with a subterranean security bunker, with the Justice Department sending a letter urging preservationists suing to block the project to drop their lawsuit. Blanche also said tightening gun laws is not the appropriate response. Democrats and some former law enforcement officials have called for bipartisan commissions on political violence and raised questions about security gaps that allowed Allen to get as close as he did to the venue.

Allen, a Caltech graduate who worked as a tutor and amateur video game developer, had traveled cross-country by rail and checked into the Hilton the day before the attack. Investigators are reviewing his writings and social media activity. Rep. Michael McCaul (R-Texas) said the Secret Service should reconsider whether the president and vice president attend the same high-profile events, and former Secret Service agents called for a reassessment of how many Cabinet officials congregate simultaneously.

stories.headline: Update: King Charles III Begins U.S. State Visit With White House Tea as Special Relationship Faces Strain
stories.summary:
King Charles III and Queen Camilla arrived in the United States on Monday for a four-day state visit, beginning with a private afternoon tea with President Donald Trump and First Lady Melania Trump at the White House — the first state visit by a British monarch since Queen Elizabeth II traveled to the U.S. in 2007. The trip is timed to coincide with the 250th anniversary of American independence from Britain and includes stops in Washington D.C., New York, and Virginia through April 30.

The visit's schedule includes a formal welcome ceremony with a 21-gun salute and military review on Tuesday, followed by a bilateral meeting between Trump and King Charles, a state dinner in the East Room, and — notably — the first address to both houses of Congress by a British monarch since Queen Elizabeth II spoke in 1991. Security concerns arising from Saturday's shooting at the White House Correspondents' Dinner briefly raised questions about the trip, but Buckingham Palace confirmed it would proceed as planned after consultations on both sides of the Atlantic.

The visit comes at a diplomatically fraught moment. Anglo-American relations have been tested by Trump administration tariffs affecting British exports, disagreements over the war in Ukraine, and broader tensions over the transatlantic alliance. UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer has been navigating a delicate balance between maintaining the 'special relationship' and managing domestic pressure over trade concessions. Trump has so far resisted significant tariff relief for Britain.

Royal watchers and diplomatic observers note the symbolism of the timing: the last British state visit to the U.S. by a sitting monarch came during the post-9/11 era of close alliance, while this one arrives amid mutual uncertainty. Rep. Ro Khanna (D-Calif.) used the occasion to urge King Charles to acknowledge victims connected to Jeffrey Epstein during his Congressional address, adding a note of political turbulence to the diplomatic pageantry.

stories.headline: At Least 42 Killed in Chad After Water Well Dispute Escalates Into Ethnic Violence
stories.summary:
At least 42 people have been killed and 10 injured in clashes between rival ethnic groups in Chad's eastern Wadi Fira province, after a dispute over a water well between two families spiraled into widespread communal violence. Authorities said the conflict triggered a cycle of reprisal attacks across a wide area, with multiple villages burned to the ground.

Chadian Deputy Prime Minister Limane Mahamat led a government delegation to the region and said Sunday that the situation had been brought under control. Officials said the government was taking measures to prevent the ongoing civil war in neighboring Sudan from further destabilizing Chad's border areas, as an influx of Sudanese refugees has increased competition over scarce resources including water and grazing land.

Deadly communal clashes are a recurring pattern in Chad, driven by competition between farmers and herders, ethnic tensions, and climate-related resource scarcity. According to the International Crisis Group, roughly 1,000 people were killed and 2,000 injured in approximately 100 such clashes between 2021 and 2024. Amnesty International has documented multiple episodes of herder-farmer violence in recent years and has criticized Chadian authorities for delayed security responses and a failure to hold perpetrators accountable, which it says fuels impunity.

The latest violence underscores the fragility of stability in the Sahel region, where climate pressures, weak state capacity, and cross-border displacement from conflicts in Sudan and elsewhere have created conditions for recurring bloodshed. A similar dispute over a well in the southwestern Dibebe region killed 33 people in November.

stories.headline: Michael Jackson Biopic Sets All-Time Box Office Record for a Biopic With $217 Million Global Opening
stories.summary:
The Michael Jackson biographical film, titled Michael, has shattered box office records with a $217 million worldwide opening since it debuted Wednesday, surpassing the previous record for a musical biopic held by Bohemian Rhapsody ($124 million in 2018) and also eclipsing Oppenheimer's $180 million opening weekend to claim the largest global debut for any biopic ever. The film stars Jackson's nephew Jaafar Jackson in the title role and uses original vocal recordings for its musical numbers.

The film has opened to a stark divide between critics and audiences: it holds a 38% score on Rotten Tomatoes from critics, several of whom described it as a sanitized portrayal of Jackson's life and career, while audiences gave it a 97% rating. Lionsgate distribution chairman Adam Fogelson credited the broad demographic appeal of the film for the record-setting numbers, saying audiences were 'clearly having a blast.'

The film's production was complicated by the rediscovery of a historic non-disclosure agreement Jackson signed with accuser Jordan Chandler in the 1990s, which included a clause prohibiting Jackson's estate from ever referencing Chandler in a film. Director Antoine Fuqua described the discovery as leading to a 'tough period' requiring significant reshoots and a reworked third act; the final film now concludes in 1988, before any abuse allegations were made, and does not include references to the child sexual abuse accusations that dogged Jackson for much of his later career. Jackson was acquitted of child molestation charges in 2005 and always maintained his innocence.

The record opening adds to a string of blockbuster musical biopics in recent years covering figures from Queen and Elton John to Elvis Presley and Bob Marley, which Hollywood has increasingly relied on as reliable commercial properties.

stories.headline: India and New Zealand Sign Free Trade Agreement Described as 'Once-in-a-Generation' Deal
stories.summary:
India and New Zealand signed a free trade agreement on Monday that slashes tariffs on the majority of goods traded between the two countries and expands market access across multiple sectors, with officials on both sides describing it as a 'once-in-a-generation' economic milestone. The deal comes as global trade patterns are being disrupted by tensions in the Middle East and the broader fallout from U.S. tariff policy under the Trump administration.

The agreement opens New Zealand's agricultural products — including dairy, meat, and wine — to India's vast consumer market on more favorable terms, while Indian goods including textiles, pharmaceuticals, and manufactured products gain improved access to New Zealand. The deal has been years in the making, with negotiations accelerating as both countries sought to diversify trade relationships amid global supply chain uncertainty.

For India, the agreement reflects Prime Minister Narendra Modi's government's broader push to negotiate bilateral trade deals with countries outside traditional blocs, following similar agreements with the UAE and Australia in recent years. New Zealand, a relatively small economy heavily dependent on agricultural exports, has prioritized market diversification, particularly as its trade relationship with China has come under geopolitical scrutiny.

The signing adds to a wave of bilateral trade activity as countries seek to lock in preferential arrangements ahead of potential further disruptions to the multilateral trading system. Analysts said the deal, while modest in scale relative to India's economy, signals New Delhi's growing willingness to engage on trade liberalization selectively and on its own terms.

stories.headline: China Merchants Group Joins Talks to Acquire CK Hutchison's Global Port Network
stories.summary:
China Merchants Group, one of China's largest state-owned port operators, has entered talks to join a consortium seeking to acquire dozens of ports owned by Hong Kong conglomerate CK Hutchison Holdings, according to people familiar with the matter. The development injects new momentum into a deal that had stalled after drawing intense scrutiny as a geopolitical flashpoint between the United States and China.

The potential sale involves CK Hutchison's sprawling network of port operations spanning multiple continents, including facilities at the Panama Canal that drew sharp attention from the Trump administration earlier this year. U.S. officials had expressed concern about Chinese-linked entities controlling strategic maritime chokepoints, and Washington applied pressure to block or reshape any transaction that would expand Beijing's influence over global shipping infrastructure.

The entry of China Merchants — a firm with deep ties to the Chinese government — into the bidding consortium is likely to reignite those concerns in Washington. Critics argue that a deal involving any state-backed Chinese buyer would undermine the original rationale for the sale, which was partly framed as an opportunity to reduce Chinese strategic leverage over key ports.

The situation highlights broader tensions over control of global trade infrastructure at a time when the U.S. and China are engaged in an escalating tariff and economic rivalry. The outcome of these negotiations could have significant implications for shipping lanes, U.S. foreign policy in Latin America, and the future of multinational port governance.

stories.headline: Army Rolls Out New Gender- and Age-Neutral Combat Field Test for Frontline Troops
stories.summary:
The U.S. Army has unveiled the Combat Field Test (CFT), a new gender- and age-neutral fitness assessment designed specifically for soldiers in combat specialties. The test is intended to mirror the physical demands of modern warfare and represents a shift toward role-specific standards rather than a universal benchmark applied across all military occupational specialties.

Soldiers must complete seven events in 30 minutes while wearing the Army Combat Uniform and boots. The course includes a one-mile run, 30 dead-stop push-ups, a 100-meter sprint, 16 lifts of a 40-pound sandbag onto a 65-inch platform, a 50-meter water-can carry, a movement drill involving a high crawl, and a final one-mile run. Army Secretary Dan Driscoll described the CFT as "a critical step forward in ensuring our soldiers serving in the most physically demanding specialties have the specific fitness required to dominate on the modern battlefield."

The announcement comes amid War Secretary Pete Hegseth's broader push to restore what he calls the "warrior ethos" within the armed forces. The Army has faced years of debate over fitness standards, including controversy over whether previous tests adequately predicted combat performance or inadvertently lowered standards by adjusting scoring based on age and gender.

The Army has rebounded from a significant recruiting shortfall — falling roughly 15,000 soldiers short of targets in 2022 — and met its 2025 goals. Officials say the new test is part of a long-term effort to improve readiness and lethality as the service modernizes for potential large-scale combat operations.

stories.headline: Trevor Bauer Throws No-Hitter for Long Island Ducks in Second U.S. Start Since 2021
stories.summary:
Former MLB Cy Young Award winner Trevor Bauer threw a no-hitter for the independent Long Island Ducks on Sunday, leading the team to a 13-0 victory over the Lancaster Stormers in a seven-inning doubleheader game. Bauer struck out seven and walked one, throwing 84 pitches in just his second U.S. start since 2021, when his major league career was effectively halted amid sexual assault allegations.

Bauer was never criminally charged, and the Los Angeles County District Attorney declined to prosecute the case. He subsequently played in Mexico's Liga Mexicana de Beisbol before returning stateside this month with the Ducks, a "Professional Partner League" affiliate of MLB. His first two outings have produced a 1.64 ERA, drawing interest from professional scouts in attendance.

An American League scout told the New York Post that Bauer showed "flashes of the guy he used to be," noting solid velocity in the 92-94 mph range and a sharp breaking ball, while acknowledging he is not yet at the level he once occupied at the top of the game. Bauer has stated his goal is an MLB return, and Sunday's performance is likely to intensify that conversation among front offices.

Bauer's case and attempted comeback remain polarizing. Supporters argue he was denied due process and deserves an opportunity to resume his career; critics contend that MLB clubs have a responsibility to consider the serious nature of the allegations regardless of the lack of criminal charges. The no-hitter ensures his name will remain prominent in baseball discussions heading into the summer.

stories.headline: Wayne Gretzky's 1988 Stanley Cup Final Jersey Sells for $2.8 Million, Setting Hockey Record
stories.summary:
A jersey worn by Wayne Gretzky during Game 4 of the 1988 Stanley Cup Final sold at auction for $2,806,000, setting a new all-time record for a hockey jersey. The previous record was also held by a Gretzky jersey from the same series, which had fetched $1.452 million in 2022, nearly doubling in value in four years.

The jersey carries particular historical significance: Game 4 of the 1988 Final between the Edmonton Oilers and Boston Bruins was famously suspended mid-game due to a power outage at Boston Garden, with the score tied 3-3. The Oilers subsequently completed a sweep two days later, winning Gretzky his fourth and final Stanley Cup. Photo-matching confirmed the jersey was worn across multiple games in that series.

Gretzky finished the 1988 postseason with 43 points in 19 games and claimed the Conn Smythe Trophy as playoff MVP. That summer, he was traded to the Los Angeles Kings in one of the most consequential transactions in sports history. The 1988 Cup run represented the final chapter of his dynasty years in Edmonton.

The sale reflects a broader surge in the high-end sports memorabilia market, which has seen record prices across multiple sports in recent years. Gretzky, who retired in 1999 with 1,963 career assists — more than 700 ahead of any other player in NHL history — remains the sport's most recognizable figure, and items linked to his career continue to command premium valuations at auction.

stories.headline: Heisman Finalist Diego Pavia Goes Undrafted, Accepts Ravens Minicamp Tryout Invite
stories.summary:
Diego Pavia, the Vanderbilt quarterback who finished second in Heisman Trophy voting last season, went undrafted through all seven rounds of the 2026 NFL Draft in Pittsburgh — becoming the first Heisman finalist since 2014 not to be selected. He has since accepted an invitation to attend the Baltimore Ravens' rookie minicamp on a tryout basis, giving him an opportunity to earn a spot on the roster.

Pavia set single-season school records at Vanderbilt with 3,539 passing yards and 29 touchdowns, guiding the Commodores to their first-ever 10-win season and earning SEC Offensive Player of the Year and the Johnny Unitas Golden Arm Award. His Heisman runner-up finish came behind Indiana's Fernando Mendoza, who was selected No. 1 overall by the Las Vegas Raiders.

The primary factor cited by NFL teams in passing on Pavia was his height. Although Vanderbilt had him listed at 6-foot, his official measurement at the NFL Scouting Combine came in at 5-foot-9 7/8, which would make him the shortest quarterback in the league. The average NFL quarterback stands 6-foot-2, though shorter quarterbacks such as Kyler Murray, listed at 5-foot-10, have found success at the position.

Pavia would face a steep climb in Baltimore, where two-time MVP Lamar Jackson is firmly entrenched as the starter. However, a strong minicamp performance could earn him an invitation to training camp this summer and keep his NFL aspirations alive.
